text: The patient is positioned supine with legs open, and the surgeon stands between the legs. The operation begins with the dissection of the ligamentum teres and falciform ligament. An exploratory laparoscopy reveals the tumor bulging from segment four, pressing towards the porta hepatis. The surgeon opens the pars flaccida of the lesser omentum, exposing the caudate lobe, and passes a forcep through the epiphyllic foramen to place a nylon tape around the porta hepatis. This tape is exteriorized through a small chest tube in the left upper quadrant for later use in external manipulation of the Pringle maneuver.

The surgeon dissects the left triangular ligament to mobilize the left lateral section, carefully avoiding the diaphragmatic veins. The hepatic cable confluence is exposed, revealing the two middle hepatic veins, along with the left and right hepatic veins as distinct structures outside the liver.

The surgeon begins dissection around the Arantius ligament to elevate the base of the left lateral section away from the inferior vena cava. The dissection progresses to the portohepatous, where the hilar plate is taken down just below the tumor. The left hepatic duct is observed to be adherent to the tumor and inseparable.

The surgeon dissects the left portal vein away from the barlact to achieve vascular control. A small vessel from segment four is ligated as it courses in front of the left hepatic duct towards the tumor. The visible capsule around the hepatocellular carcinoma allows operation close to the tumor along natural tissue planes. The left hepatic artery is dissected, double clip ligated, and divided.

The surgeon continues dissection along the groove for the ligamentum venosum, identifying and ligating a small hepatic artery. The dissection occurs between the inferior vena cava and the tumor, which is being elevated from the inferior vena cava while staying within the areolar tissue.

The surgeon mobilizes tissue in front of the inferior vena cava towards the insertion of the left and middle hepatic veins. A stapling device is used to ligate and divide the left portal vein. Dissection continues lateral to the left hepatic vein, revealing the middle hepatic vein arching over the forceps as the surgeon navigates the groove between the middle hepatic vein insertion and the vena cava.

The surgeon identifies a small, short hepatic vein leading towards the tumor in front of the inferior vena cava. This vein is clipped and divided using surgical instruments. The procedure is filmed using a 3D laparoscope, providing a high-definition view of the surgical field.

The surgeon works anterior to the right anterior pedicle and the bifurcation of the barlux, noting the adherence of the barlux to the tumor. The dissection progresses lateral and posterior to the right anterior pedicle to develop the junction between the right anterior and right posterior pedicles. The right posterior portal vein and possibly the right posterior artery are visible in front of the pedicle.

The surgeon dissects the biliary confluence, attempting to develop a plane between the tumor and the biliary confluence to identify the right posterior duct. Due to poor quality biliary images from preoperative MRCP, the tumor compresses the area, complicating visualization. A smaller right angle instrument is used to manipulate the tissues, inadvertently creating a hole in the left bile duct. Despite challenges in identifying the right posterior duct, the surgeon explores the left duct further.

The surgeon attempts to locate the right posterior duct near the confluence but is unable to define it. The left hepatic duct is completely divided, and clips are placed on the stump. The right anterior duct is further mobilized, revealing a clear tissue plane that is followed without breaching the tumor capsule.

The surgeon divides the remaining sheath around the left duct, increasing its mobility, but no right posterior duct is visible. The portal vein is clearly separable from the tumor and the posterior plate. Cholangiograms are performed through the cystic duct with a bulldog clamp on the right anterior duct, but no posterior duct is visualized, suggesting it enters the left hepatic duct.

The surgeon demonstrates the posterior duct, which is anomalously positioned away from the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts. The confluence of the bile duct and the right hepatic duct is sacrificed to achieve a better surgical margin.

The surgeon places a bulldog clamp across the right anterior portal vein and artery, creating a clear demarcation line between liver segments five and six. Despite camera clashing, the line extends up to segments seven and eight. The right posterior duct is divided while maintaining the tumor capsule intact. The corner next to the gallbladder between segments five and six is opened without using a Pringle maneuver.

The surgeon uses an energy device to open the first half centimeter of the liver parenchyma, addressing minor bleeding. With increased mobility, dissection proceeds behind the hepatic veins. The left lateral section of the liver is visibly folded over, and the left hepatic vein is being dissected.

The surgeon positions the left lateral section to expose the middle hepatic vein and the left hepatic vein. The left hepatic vein is identified swinging up and over, separate from the middle hepatic vein. A stapling device with a vascular load is carefully positioned and fired to divide the left hepatic vein. With the left lateral section elevated, the left side of the middle hepatic vein becomes more visible. Remaining parenchymal tissue between the stump of the left hepatic vein and the middle hepatic vein is gently dissected away, revealing the groove between the two middle hepatic veins from a lateral view.

A right angle clamp is passed behind the leftmost middle hepatic vein, which is then divided using a stapling device with a vascular load. The Pringle Maneuver is applied to control blood flow during parenchymal transection, reducing blood loss and improving visualization of structures. Laparoscopic tools assist with dissection, and an energy device seals small vessels as they are identified.

The surgeon dissects a tributary of the radiopathic vein from segment five, temporarily removing the Pringle maneuver. The vessel is clipped and divided, while moisture occasionally splatters on the camera, requiring cleaning.

The surgeon works on the anterior pedicle, developing a plane behind it in preparation for transection using a stapling device. The stapling device is placed, and the bulldog clamp and clip on the right hepatic duct are mobilized out of the way before firing. Parenchymal transection is performed with the patient rotated slightly to the left side. Extra posterior liver tissue is taken to improve the margin, although the capsule around the tumor remains intact.

A nylon tape is wrapped around the future specimen, serving as a visual guide rather than a hanging maneuver, to maintain alignment during dissection and ensure the right hepatic vein is accurately located.

The surgeon divides the liver capsule, allowing increased mobility of the future specimen. The specimen is lifted forward, revealing the right hepatic vein. Tissues are carefully teased off the right hepatic vein while maintaining the pseudocapsule with the specimen.

The surgeon uses a Thunder bead to take small tributaries within the liver parenchyma between the right hepatic vein and the right middle hepatic vein, which is visible and being dissected. A small bleeder on the right hepatic vein is noted but controlled due to pneumoperitoneum. The right middle hepatic vein, thicker than adjacent veins, joins the stump of the left middle hepatic vein. A stapling device is used to transect the major vessel, while a clip and energy device are applied to another vein extending into the specimen. The dissection proceeds behind the tumor into normal liver parenchyma using a clamp crush technique.

The surgeon identifies small tributaries of the right portal vein and right hepatic vein, noting a bridge of tissue away from the tumor. The tissue is thinned down further, allowing the introduction of a stapling device to complete the transection with one more fire of the stapler.

The specimen is placed in a retrieval bag but remains in the surgical field. The operating table is leveled, and additional fluid is administered to the patient. Minor oozing from the right hepatic vein is observed and controlled with local pressure. The surgeon explores the right posterior duct in preparation for hepaticojejunostomy, applying flow seal to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon prepares the Roux-Y limb after identifying the ligament of Treitz, dividing the bowel using a vascular stapler to prevent staple line bleeding. The biliary limb is measured to fifty-five to sixty centimeters, and the defect left by the stapler is closed. The biliary limb is brought forward and held with a laparoscopic grasper, then passed behind the transverse colon through the transverse mesocolon. A small midline incision is made for the hepaticojejunostomy, which is performed using an interrupted technique.
